template_name: Standard Prescription Format – Benign Neonatal Rash Counseling
principles: Most neonatal rashes are benign and self-limiting

general_care.details: Do not apply talcum powder, scented oils, or creams
Keep baby warm; avoid excessive exposure

condition_specific_guidance.management: No treatment needed; resolves in few days

management_plan: Follow-up only if symptoms persist or worsen
counselling: Most newborn rashes are harmless and self-resolving — ज़्यादातर नवजात के दाने हानिरहित होते हैं और अपने आप ठीक हो जाते हैं
No need for creams, antibiotics, or medications — किसी क्रीम, एंटीबायोटिक या दवा की ज़रूरत नहीं है
Avoid talcum powder and scented products — टैल्कम पाउडर और खुशबूदार चीज़ों से बचें
Maintain hygiene with plain lukewarm water bathing — गुनगुने पानी से साफ़ सफ़ाई रखें
Rash will gradually resolve over days to weeks — दाने धीरे-धीरे कुछ दिनों से हफ़्तों में ठीक हो जाएंगे
Seek medical care if red flag symptoms appear — अगर खतरे के संकेत दिखें तो तुरंत डॉक्टर को दिखाएं
warning_signs: Fever with rash — दाने के साथ बुखार
Blisters or pus-filled bumps — छाले या मवाद वाले दाने
Rash spreading rapidly — दाने तेज़ी से फैलना
Baby not feeding well — बच्चे का ठीक से दूध न पीना
Baby becoming lethargic or irritable — बच्चे का सुस्त या चिड़चिड़ा हो जाना
Purple or dark red spots on skin — त्वचा पर बैंगनी या गहरे लाल धब्बे
expected_course: Most benign neonatal rashes appear within the first 1–2 weeks of life and resolve spontaneously within days to weeks without any treatment. Erythema toxicum typically appears on day 1–3 and resolves by 5–14 days. Milia resolve within 4 weeks. Neonatal acne may take 1–3 months to clear.
key_clinical_points: Erythema toxicum is the most common benign neonatal rash — seen in up to 50% of term neonates
Milia are tiny white papules on the nose and face — do not squeeze
Neonatal acne is hormonal and self-limiting
Transient neonatal pustular melanosis is more common in dark-skinned neonates
Cutis marmorata is a normal vasomotor response to cold
Key differentiator: benign rashes occur in well, afebrile neonates with good feeding
Investigate only if systemic signs (fever, poor feeding, hepatosplenomegaly) are present
Avoid unnecessary topical steroids or antibiotics

severity_assessment.mild: Localized rash, well baby, feeding normally, afebrile — observation and reassurance only
severity_assessment.moderate: Widespread rash but baby otherwise well — close observation, consider dermatology review if persistent beyond 2 weeks
severity_assessment.severe: Rash with fever, vesicles/bullae, poor feeding, lethargy, or systemic signs — investigate for infection (CBC, CRP, blood culture, skin swab), consider NICU admission

monitoring_parameters.frequency: Daily by parents, at each OPD visit
If any new rash or systemic concern
